# Week 2: Qiskit Programming

### Quantum States and Operations in Qiskit

- [Music] welcome back to the nptel course on quantum computing this week you will learn how to program quantum computers but before going there there are a few more quantum computing concepts that you should know last week you learned all about superposition and how to harness superposition states into building basic quantum circuits there are other quantum mechanical concepts such as entanglement and interference which are key to building quantum algorithms and those are the concepts that we will be discussing today we will discuss qubit entanglement and what are known as bell states we will then discuss bell measurement and the phenomenon of quantum teleportation we will briefly discuss the phenomenon of interference before providing an intuition about how to construct quantum algorithms before we dive into this lecture let's do a quick recap on what we learned last week which including concepts and notations that are going to be necessary to understand this particular lecture you learned all about single cubics and how to represent uh qubits in dirac notation as kets you learned about measurement and the idea that a single qubit when measured collapses either to the basis state zero or the basis state one and the sum of those probabilities must add to one and you learned how to measure these probabilities using the bracket operation you also learned uh how to build a uh or a single qubit gate or what its representation is in linear algebraic terms uh you can as you can see here a qubit gate is nothing but a linear transformation or a matrix and it can also be expressed in terms of outer products uh in the daylight notation you learned that a single qubit circuit is nothing but a sequence of dates applied to a particular input qubit and uh the measurement of the output qubit uh which has a particular probability of being in the state 0 or 1. next we saw how we can represent a state consisting of multiple qubits or what we call a multipartized state and mathematically this is the tensor product of several single qubits a multiple qubit gate is a gate applied to multiple qubits at the same time to produce an equal number of qubits in the output and the principle of reversibility applies to such gates which states that if we know the outputs and we know the gate function or the linear transformation then we can recover the inputs you saw how we can apply hadamard transforms to individual qubits in a multi-qubit gate to produce an exponential size increase in state space that is to produce a 2 to the power and straight space from just n cubits and you saw how the hadamard gate that represents this transform on an end state input is a 2 to the power n by 2 to the power n matrix and that's the tensor product of hadamard gates applied n times with each other you saw how this particular gate can take an input in the basis state that is every input qubit is in the basis state 0 and produce a superposition state consisting of 2 to the power n possible states that is each of the possible uh combinations of the input qubits q1 up to qn and each of those states are in equal probability and when we have this 2 to the power inside sheet space we can do a lot of useful computation with this much more rapidly than we can using classic computer now let's move to the main topic of today's lecture entanglement if you remember from last week's lecture a multi-qubit state can be classified broadly into product states and entangled states product states being those that can be expressed as the tensor product of two defined uh states like q1 and q2 whereas entangled states are those that cannot be expressed as a tensor product of any two given states wealth states are four two qubit states that are maximally entangled and here are the four best states uh the best state psi zero zero is the state that you see here it's uh it just has the

qubits away from each other but if they happen to be entangled they are going to remain entangled however far remove those cubes from each other and you see a very interesting example of that when you
